text: Wow, this has a ton of potential. A whole new way of doing programming. But I spent about an hour trying to do some simple stuff with it and it’s pretty excruciating to use in its current state. I’d definitely be interested in playing with a somewhat more polished version of this tool.

Wow, this has a ton of potential. A whole new way of doing programming. But I spent about an hour trying to do some simple stuff with it and it’s pretty excruciating to use in its current state. I’d definitely be interested in playing with a somewhat more polished version of this tool.

<@UDCLT064X> Thanks for your kind words.

I have to admit, your experience using the live demo makes me wonder if I should have put it online at all. PANE is /almost/ usable by me. But I have put absolutely no effort into making it usable by anyone other than me. That's because the live demo isn't intended to be the sharable product of the research – the presentation is.

I wanted to put the live demo online so that adventurous folk could mess with it. But now I worry that people may approach it with the wrong expectations, and waste their time or come away with what would be, in my opinion, an unfairly negative opinion of the project.

It doesn't sound like that was your experience. (Phew!) But I'm worried.

(There's another version of this set of worries for the further step of posting the source code. As you can imagine, "usability by anyone other than me" drops exponentially from "presentation" to "live demo" to "source code".)

I think posting the live demo is great. It proves that this is an actual product rather than just an idea. In fact I think you should share the source code as well even if it’s rough and hacky. Everyone understands that this is a work-in-progress.

Yeah that’s a good point, certainly complicates it. I don’t have a solution but often times identifying problems is more important :) let me know if anything else comes to mind.

Welcome! I for one would be interested in hearing more about your project, as I haven't really found a sustainable note-taking system I'm happy with

> Proto definitions would commonly expand to a hundred fields, all optional. So one often couldn’t look at another team’s proto definition and easily deduce the constraints, such as which fields had to exist together or be used disjointedly.
This is why API descriptions desperately need to embrace sum types (aka “variants” aka “tagged unions”). I’m still waiting for sum types to become more widely discovered outside of the FP community. JSON of course doesn’t have any first-class concept of sum types. Cap’n Proto does though (calls them “unions”), and it looks like you can do sums in Protocol Buffer using `oneof`.

> Proto definitions would commonly expand to a hundred fields, all optional. So one often couldn’t look at another team’s proto definition and easily deduce the constraints, such as which fields had to exist together or be used disjointedly.
This is why API descriptions desperately need to embrace sum types (aka “variants” aka “tagged unions”). I’m still waiting for sum types to become more widely discovered outside of the FP community. JSON of course doesn’t have any first-class concept of sum types. Cap’n Proto does though (calls them “unions”), and it looks like you can do sums in Protocol Buffer using `oneof`.

Yes, newer systems use `oneof`. But a) we still have lots of legacy definitions, b) mistakes are hard to undo so you're stuck with them, and c) The situation is sometimes more complex than a simple `oneof`.

I should add that my experience is anecdotal, and a year old at this point.

Me either! I feel like it's such a hard problem, that you need the freedom to tinker on your own system as you're using it
but the dev workflow afforded by the normal web stack is too cumbersome
